title: Weekly Reflections
text: This page documents my weekly reflections for EPPS 6323 Knowledge Mining, Spring 2026. Each entry responds to the prompts assigned for that week. These are personal reflections — not polished academic writing. I am a PhD student from Bangladesh. English is my third language. I write the way I think.

title: Weekly Reflections
text:
Week 3

February 10, 2026


AI and Originality; What is AGI?
AI and Originality
I have a confession. Half of my literature notes last week were written with Claude. It was good. Maybe too good. That scared me a little.
Originality is not just about having a new idea. It is about having your idea. AI echoes what already exists. It is fast and comprehensive, but it is not original.
Perhaps the real threat is not that AI replaces our ideas. It is that we stop bothering to have them. That is what worries me most.
What is AGI?
AGI — Artificial General Intelligence — can do anything a human mind can do. We are not there yet. But the direction seems clear enough to be unsettling.
In public policy research, so much depends on judgment. Why does a government choose one redistribution strategy over another? That is not a pattern recognition problem. It requires context, history, and intuition.
I could be wrong about all of this. And that uncertainty might be the most honest thing I can say about AGI right now.

title: Weekly Reflections
text:
Week 4

February 17, 2026


Using AI in My Research
How to Start Using AI for My Project
Starting was the hardest part. I did not know where AI fit into my research. Should it help me read? Write? Code? All of the above?
Eventually I stopped overthinking it. I started small. I asked Claude to help me understand a paper I was struggling with. It worked. That was the beginning.
Now AI is part of almost every stage of my workflow. It is especially useful for brainstorming and polishing my language. English is my third language. Having an AI that can clean up my writing without losing my meaning is, honestly, a relief.
Claude and ChatGPT: Two Different Tools
I use them differently. Claude is my coding partner. It is cleaner, more accurate, and better at catching logical errors. When I am writing R code for my labor policy analysis, Claude is my first stop.
ChatGPT is better for creative tasks. Slides, visual summaries, brainstorming research angles. It has a certain flair that Claude does not always match.
Perhaps the best way to put it: Claude makes my work rigorous. ChatGPT makes it presentable.

title: Weekly Reflections
text:
Week 5

February 24, 2026


Knowledge, Information, and AI Failures
How Do Humans Find Knowledge?
Information is everywhere. Knowledge is rare.
When I read that Bangladesh’s remittance inflows increased by 15% last year, that is information. When I understand why it happened, what policy conditions enabled it, and what it means for redistribution, that is knowledge.
Humans find knowledge through context, experience, and judgment. We connect dots that data alone cannot connect. That is perhaps what makes human researchers still relevant in an age of AI.
A List of AI Failures. Any Pattern?
I have collected enough AI failures to write a small comedy.
Hallucinated citations. This one is dangerous. AIs cite papers that do not exist. Confidently. Beautifully formatted. Completely fabricated. I learned this the hard way. Now I download and read every single article AI references before I use it. Every single one.
Faulty code. I have lost hundreds of hours to this. AI writes code that looks right but does not run. I ask for a fix. The fix is also wrong. I ask again. Still wrong. It is like asking for directions from someone who has never left the house.
Outdated facts argued with confidence. My favorite example: I asked ChatGPT about Bangladesh’s political situation in 2025. It told me Sheikh Hasina was still Prime Minister. She was ousted in August 2024 following a mass uprising. When I corrected it, ChatGPT argued with me. I was so amused I took a screenshot and posted it on Facebook.
The pattern? AI fails most when it is most confident. That is worth remembering every time we are tempted to trust it without verification.

title: Weekly Reflections
text:
Week 6

March 3, 2026


My First ML Pipeline: What Accuracy Does Not Tell You
Starting the Pipeline
My first ML pipeline was simple. I tried to predict whether someone voted for Tsai Ing-wen in Taiwan’s 2016 election using age, education, and income.
I started with OLS regression. It ran. It looked fine. Then I realized the dependent variable only takes two values — 0 or 1. OLS was predicting negative probabilities. That is not fine.
I switched to logistic regression. Accuracy came out at 63.1%. Looked okay. It was not.
What Accuracy Did Not Tell Me
The confusion matrix was embarrassing. The model was calling almost everyone a Tsai voter. It was not learning anything. It was just making one lazy guess repeatedly.
Accuracy did not catch this. Error analysis did.
This matters for policy work. If a model flags everyone as high-risk, the accuracy might still look acceptable. But the policy would be useless.
Overfitting: Knowledge vs. Noise
Overfitting is when a model memorizes the data instead of learning from it. It works great on training data. It fails everywhere else.
I think of it this way. Noise is random. Knowledge is pattern. An overfit model cannot tell the difference. It treats both as equally important.
For redistribution and labor policy research, this is a real problem. A model built on noise will give wrong answers the moment real-world conditions change slightly. And in policy, conditions always change.

title: Weekly Reflections
text:
Week 7

March 24, 2026


Prediction vs. Explanation; A Causal Diagram from My Research
Prediction vs. Explanation: When Does It Matter?
Sometimes knowing what will happen is enough. A hospital predicting which patients will miss appointments does not need to know why. It just needs to call them.
But in policy research, why almost always matters. If I know that employment protection laws reduce labor productivity, that is useful. But if I do not know why, I cannot design a better policy. Is it moral hazard? Is it reduced firm investment? Is it something else entirely? The answer changes everything.
My MPSA paper is fundamentally about explanation, not prediction. I could have built a model that predicts productivity drops in certain states. But that would not tell policymakers anything actionable. The point was to isolate the mechanism — the Good Faith exception specifically — and show that it weakens the dismissal threat, which then reduces worker effort. That causal story is what makes the finding useful.
A Causal Diagram from My Research
The core relationship in my paper is:
Good Faith Exception → Lower Labor Productivity
But this relationship does not exist in a vacuum. Several confounders could explain both the adoption of the law and the productivity change:

State economic conditions — struggling economies may adopt protective laws and have lower productivity
Industry composition — R&D-heavy states may look different from manufacturing states
Firm size — large firms adapt more easily to legal changes
Political environment — more democratic states may adopt both protective laws and different economic policies

This is exactly why I used a staggered Difference-in-Differences design. The laws were adopted by state courts at different times, not by legislatures responding to economic conditions. That staggered adoption is what makes the treatment plausibly exogenous.
The Weibull hazard model confirmed this. Lagged state productivity did not predict when a state adopted the Good Faith exception. The law arrived independently of economic trends. That is what separates a causal claim from a simple predictive association — the treatment was not chosen because of the outcome.

title: Weekly Reflections
text:
Week 8

March 31, 2026


Text Mining, NLP, LLMs, and a Guide for Using AI in Research
How Can Text Mining Reveal Patterns That Close Reading Cannot?
I cannot read 113 files carefully. Nobody can. Text mining can.
When I ran the APSA jobs corpus, it found that “public policy” and “assistant professor” appeared together nearly 100,000 times across the files. I would never have caught that by reading.
But text mining does not understand anything. It counts words. It cannot tell if “diversity” in a job ad is genuine or just legal cover. That part is still my job.
How Can NLP and LLM Assist My Research?
A few concrete ideas I have actually thought about.
My paper is about state court decisions and labor productivity. I could use topic modeling to track how judicial language around the Good Faith exception changed over decades. That would take me years to do manually.
For my Bangladesh remittance research, I could analyze Bangladesh Bank policy documents to see how the language around remittance policy shifted over time. I work there. I have those documents. This feels very doable.
Claude helped me translate dense legal text into plain language when I was building my theoretical framework. That alone saved me significant time.
Limitations and Suggested Solutions
Three that I have personally experienced.
Hallucination. AI cites papers that do not exist. My fix is boring but necessary — I check every single citation myself.
Context blindness. NLP does not know what words mean politically. In Bangladesh policy documents, certain phrases carry weight that no algorithm would catch. Domain knowledge still matters.
Language bias. Most NLP tools work best in English. My Bangladesh research involves Bengali documents. Multilingual models exist but they are less reliable. I do not have a good solution for this yet.
An AI for Research Guide: Be Careful
A few honest rules I follow.
Know your question before asking AI. It will answer confidently even if the question is wrong.
Verify every citation. Every single one.
Test every line of code. AI code looks clean and breaks quietly.
Disclose AI use. Always. No exceptions.
Do not let AI do your thinking. It can summarize. It cannot tell you what matters for your specific question. That is your job and nobody else’s.
Hinton warned that we might outsource our thinking and forget how to think. I take that seriously.

title: Weekly Reflections
text:
Week 9

April 7, 2026


What LLMs Do Well, Where They Fail, and What Comes Next
What LLMs Do Well and Where They Fail
LLMs are remarkable at certain things. Summarizing long texts. Cleaning up writing. Explaining complicated concepts in plain language. For someone like me — working in a third language — that last one matters a lot.
But they fail in ways that are hard to predict. And that unpredictability is the real problem.
My favorite example is not academic. I asked ChatGPT about Bangladesh’s political situation in early 2025. It told me Sheikh Hasina was still Prime Minister. She was ousted in August 2024 following a mass uprising. I corrected it. It argued with me. Confidently. I was so amused I took a screenshot and posted it on Facebook.
Funny story. But the same failure mode shows up in serious research contexts too.
When I was working on my MPSA paper on employment protection and labor productivity, I asked Claude to suggest relevant citations on wrongful discharge laws. It gave me a list. Beautifully formatted. Proper APA style. Several of them did not exist. One paper had the right authors but the wrong journal, wrong year, and wrong title. It looked real enough that I almost used it.
Now I check everything. Every single citation. That is my personal tax for using AI in research.
How Generative AI Will Reshape Public Policy Research
In five years, I think certain research tasks will look very different.
Literature reviews will be almost fully automated. What takes me weeks will take minutes. That is genuinely exciting. It also means the bar for original contribution will rise. If anyone can survey a literature instantly, the value shifts to asking better questions.
Data collection will change too. NLP tools will make it easier to analyze policy documents, court decisions, legislative texts at scale. For my research on wrongful discharge laws, this could mean tracking judicial language across all 50 states over decades — something that is practically impossible today.
But some things should stay human.
Causal identification is one. Deciding whether a research design actually isolates a treatment effect requires judgment that I do not trust AI to make. My staggered DiD design for the WDL paper involved dozens of small decisions about sample construction, control variables, and interpretation. Those decisions reflect theoretical commitments. AI does not have those.
Policy interpretation is another. Numbers from a regression do not speak for themselves. Saying that Good Faith exception adoption reduces productivity by 53% relative to median EBIT is a finding. Explaining what that means for workers, firms, and policymakers — that requires human judgment, institutional knowledge, and ethical responsibility.
AI can help me find the pattern. It cannot tell me what the pattern means. That part stays with me.

title: Weekly Reflections
text:
Week 10

April 14, 2026


What is RAG and Why Does It Matter?
What is RAG and Why Does It Matter?
RAG stands for Retrieval-Augmented Generation. The name sounds complicated. The idea is not.
A regular LLM only knows what it was trained on. Ask it about something recent and it either guesses or gets it wrong. Ask it about Sheikh Hasina and it will tell you she is still Prime Minister.
RAG fixes this by giving the model access to external documents before it answers. Instead of guessing, it retrieves relevant information first and then generates a response based on what it actually found. It is like the difference between asking someone a question from memory versus letting them look it up first.
For research, this matters. A RAG system grounded in actual policy documents or court decisions would be far more reliable than a regular LLM making things up. The answer is connected to a real source. That is a meaningful improvement.
Recent Criticisms and Suggestions
RAG is not perfect though. A few criticisms I have come across:
It is only as good as what it retrieves. If the retrieved documents are wrong or irrelevant, the answer will be too. Garbage in, garbage out.
It can still hallucinate. Even with retrieved documents, the model sometimes ignores them and makes things up anyway.
It struggles with complex questions that require reasoning across multiple documents.
Suggested improvements I have seen discussed: better retrieval methods, teaching models to say “I don’t know” when retrieved documents are insufficient, and combining RAG with human verification for high-stakes outputs.
That last one sounds familiar. It is basically what I already do manually.

title: Weekly Reflections
text:
Week 11

April 28, 2026


Knowledge Graphs and My Research
What is a Knowledge Graph?
A traditional database stores data in rows and columns. It is good at answering simple questions. How many firms adopted the Good Faith exception? What is the average productivity?
A vector store is different. It stores text as numbers and finds things that are similar in meaning. Useful for search.
A knowledge graph does something neither can do well. It stores relationships between things. Not just what exists, but how things connect to each other.
That difference matters. A database can tell me that a firm is in Texas and Texas adopted the Good Faith exception in 1985. But it cannot easily tell me how that legal change rippled through the firm’s hiring decisions, productivity, and financial leverage all at once. A knowledge graph can.
Three Entities and Three Relationships from My Research
My MPSA paper gives me a natural starting point.
Entities:

Wrongful Discharge Laws
Firms
Labor Productivity

Relationships:

Wrongful Discharge Laws constrain Firms
Firms generate Labor Productivity
Wrongful Discharge Laws reduce Labor Productivity

This seems simple. But even this small graph can answer questions a flat dataset cannot.
For example — which firms were most affected by WDL adoption? A flat dataset gives me a coefficient. A knowledge graph lets me trace the path. It shows that large firms adapted through temporary staffing, which buffered the productivity effect. Smaller firms could not do that. The relationship tells a richer story than the number alone.
That is perhaps the most useful thing about knowledge graphs. They do not just store answers. They store the logic connecting the answers.

title: Weekly Reflections
text:
Week 12

May 5, 2026


AI for Science and Agentic AI
What Does “AI for Science” Mean?
AI for Science means using AI not just to write or summarize, but to actually help do research. Finding patterns in data. Generating hypotheses. Running analyses. It is AI as a research tool, not just a writing tool.
For someone like me, that distinction matters. I already use Claude and ChatGPT to clean up my writing. That is useful but not transformative. AI for Science is something bigger. It is about using AI to do things that would otherwise take months.
How Agentic AI Changes the Research Workflow
A regular AI interaction is simple. I ask a question. It answers. That is it.
Agentic AI is different. It can plan. It can use tools. It can take multiple steps without me asking each time.
Let me use my MPSA paper as an example. That paper required several distinct tasks. Downloading Compustat data. Cleaning 91,974 firm-year observations. Running staggered difference-in-differences regressions. Checking parallel trends. Running entropy balancing. Writing it all up.
I did each of these steps separately. Manually. It took months.
An agentic AI system could potentially handle several of these steps in sequence. It could download the data, flag missing observations, suggest appropriate control variables, and run robustness checks — all without me prompting each step individually.
That is not science fiction. It is already partially possible.
But I am cautious about one thing. The decisions in my paper were not just technical. Choosing the Good Faith exception as the primary treatment variable was a theoretical decision. Deciding to use EBIT per employee instead of other productivity measures was a judgment call. Interpreting the 53% productivity decline required institutional knowledge about how U.S. labor law actually works.
Agentic AI can run the analysis. It cannot make those judgments. At least not yet.
So for now, the workflow I imagine is this. AI handles the mechanical steps. I handle the thinking. That is perhaps the most honest description of where we are in 2026.







TipAI Use Disclosure



These reflections were written with assistance from Claude (Anthropic) and ChatGPT (OpenAI). These tools helped with language editing and drafting. English is my third language and AI assistance helped me express my ideas more clearly.
All research examples, personal experiences, and opinions are my own. The Sheikh Hasina example is real. The citation failures are real. The frustration with broken code is very real.
Final decisions on content and argument were made by me. AI did not generate the ideas — it helped me write them down.

title: Assignment 2: Prompt exercise
text:
Step 1: Initial Prompt Creation

To establish a functional starting point, I am starting with a “Baseline Prompt.” This prompt is intentionally standard and simple. It requests the core components of a systematic review—methodology, synthesis, and hypothesis—but withholds specific instructions which are left for future refinement.

title: Assignment 2: Prompt exercise
text:
1. 1. AI models employed

ChatGPT 5.4 (High Reasoning Mode): OpenAI’s latest iteration, selected for its advanced logical structuring and “Thinking” capabilities released in early 2026.
Copilot (GPT-5.4 Turbo Engine): Microsoft’s implementation, which leverages the GPT-5.4 backbone integrated with real-time web-indexing via the March 2026 update.
Grok 4.20 (Expert Mode): xAI’s current flagship. I have bypassed the now-legacy Grok 3 in favor of the 4.20 “Heavy” architecture, which utilizes a multi-agent “Reality Engine” far superior to its 2025 predecessor.

title: Assignment 2: Prompt exercise
text:
1. 2. The Baseline Prompt
“Conduct a 2,000-word structured systematic literature review on the applications of data mining and machine learning in Public Policy. Include a methodology section describing search strategy and inclusion criteria, synthesize key findings, identify emerging trends and research gaps, and propose one testable hypothesis. Use a formal academic tone and follow systematic review standards.”

title: Assignment 2: Prompt exercise
text:
1. 3. Baseline Outputs
The following sections contain the raw, unedited responses from the three AI models. These outputs serve as the data for my subsequent analysis and refinement.

ChatGPT 5.4



Conversational MetadataOutput (baseline Prompt)



Note: Before generating the formal text, the model provided the following self-correction and reasoning regarding the available literature on individual productivity.

I’m pulling together a current evidence base and will structure this like a real review rather than a dressed-up summary: search logic, screening rules, synthesis, gaps, and a hypothesis at the end.
A pattern is already showing through the fog: most of the literature clusters around prediction, targeting, and text analysis, while far fewer studies evaluate whether these tools actually improve policy outcomes in the wild.
Below is a PRISMA-informed structured systematic literature review on the applications of data mining and machine learning in public policy. I should be precise about one thing: because I did not run a subscription-database search inside Scopus or Web of Science directly, this is best understood as a systematic-style review based on a transparent, reproducible web search of scholarly and institutional sources, rather than a formally registered full systematic review. The structure, screening logic, and synthesis follow systematic review standards.



Applications of Data Mining and Machine Learning in Public Policy: A Structured Systematic Literature Review

Introduction

The use of data mining and machine learning (ML) in public policy has moved from the margins to the engine room of governance. Governments and policy researchers now use these methods to predict need, target beneficiaries, detect fraud, analyze policy texts, forecast risks, and evaluate interventions. OECD reporting shows that governments increasingly deploy AI and related analytics in public service design, delivery, and evaluation, while the public administration literature treats AI adoption as a major institutional shift rather than a narrow technical upgrade.
At the same time, the field is not tidy. “Machine learning in public policy” covers at least four distinct activities: prediction, classification, text analysis, and causal policy evaluation. These are often conflated, even though the methodological stakes differ sharply. A model that predicts poverty for aid targeting is not doing the same job as a model that estimates heterogeneous treatment effects in policy evaluation, and neither is equivalent to topic modeling of legal texts. The literature increasingly recognizes that predictive accuracy alone is not enough; fairness, interpretability, transparency, and welfare consequences matter just as much in the public sector.
This review asks: How are data mining and machine learning being applied in public policy, what findings recur across domains, what trends are emerging, and where are the major gaps?

Methodology

This review followed a PRISMA-informed approach to transparency in search, selection, and reporting. PRISMA 2020 emphasizes explicit reporting of search strategy, selection criteria, and synthesis decisions, even when the review is not a meta-analysis.
2.1 Search strategy
A structured web search was conducted on April 2, 2026 using combinations of the following keywords: machine learning, data mining, public policy, public administration, policy evaluation, text mining, algorithmic transparency, welfare targeting, fraud detection, criminal justice, public health, and government. Searches were run through Google Scholar indexing and publisher or institutional domains including BMJ, Nature, Wiley, Springer, ScienceDirect, NBER, OECD, PubMed/PMC, and related scholarly repositories. Representative searches also targeted subdomains such as social protection, public health, environmental compliance, education policy, tax administration, and criminal justice.
2.2 Inclusion criteria
Studies were included if they:


examined applications of data mining, machine learning, or closely related AI methods in a public policy or public-sector context;


were published in peer-reviewed journals, major working paper series, or authoritative institutional reports;


offered either empirical findings, methodological contributions relevant to policy, or synthetic review frameworks;


were available in English.


2.3 Exclusion criteria
Studies were excluded if they:


focused only on private-sector business analytics without public-policy relevance;


were purely technical model-development papers with no governance, policy, or public-sector application;


were opinion pieces without substantive empirical or conceptual contribution;


duplicated the same finding in a weaker source when a stronger primary source existed.


2.4 Selection logic and synthesis
Priority was given to recent reviews, foundational methodological papers, and high-salience empirical applications. The final synthesis is thematic rather than statistical because the literature is heterogeneous in data type, policy setting, outcome definitions, and validation standards. That heterogeneity makes a pooled quantitative meta-analysis inappropriate here.

Results: Main Areas of Application

3.1 Targeting and allocation in social policy
One of the clearest public-policy applications of ML is beneficiary targeting. Aiken and coauthors show that machine learning using mobile phone metadata can improve the targeting of humanitarian or anti-poverty assistance relative to some conventional alternatives. In Togo, ML-based targeting reduced exclusion errors compared with geographic targeting and improved welfare performance under emergency aid allocation. Later work in Bangladesh extends the comparison across proxy means testing, community-based targeting, and digital-data approaches, showing that algorithmic targeting can be more scalable and sometimes more cost-effective, though performance depends on scale, program design, and administrative constraints.
This branch of the literature matters because it moves ML beyond mere prediction toward policy design under scarcity. Battiston and colleagues sharpen that point by arguing that prediction-based policies should be assessed according to welfare consequences, not just standard classification metrics such as AUC or accuracy. That is a crucial correction. A model can be statistically impressive and still be normatively poor if its errors fall disproportionately on high-need populations.
3.2 Public health and population-level policy
Public health is another major application area. Reviews in population and public health show that ML is being used for outbreak surveillance, disease burden forecasting, intervention assessment, and resource allocation. These applications are especially attractive when policy environments involve high-dimensional, rapidly changing data. Predictive modeling in population health has been framed as useful for anticipating future disease burden and informing preventive intervention, while more recent reviews argue that ML can expand the boundaries of conventional public health policy evaluation.
Yet the public health literature also serves as a warning label. Even where predictive performance is strong, concerns recur around data quality, digital infrastructure, privacy, and unequal model performance across demographic groups. In other words, public health has become both the showcase and the stress test of algorithmic governance.
3.3 Text mining for policy analysis and agenda mapping
A distinct and rapidly growing line of work uses text mining, NLP, and topic modeling to analyze policy documents, news, consultation materials, and administrative texts. Gyódi demonstrates how text mining can support problem recognition and policy issue selection by identifying emerging themes and regulatory concerns in large textual corpora. Other studies use AI and text mining to analyze education policy, government procurement policy, urban greening policy, and policy diffusion intensity. The value here is not only speed; text mining enables policy scholars to turn diffuse textual archives into measurable indicators of issue salience, framing, and diffusion.
This area also blurs the line between scholarship and policy practice. ML is being used not only to study policy but to build policy intelligence systems—tools that help agencies monitor discourse, detect implementation gaps, and map administrative priorities over time. That trend is likely to accelerate as large language models and modern embedding methods become easier to deploy.
3.4 Fraud detection, tax administration, and integrity systems
Another strong application area is public integrity: fraud detection, compliance monitoring, and audit selection. In tax administration, recent work finds that ML-guided audit selection can substantially improve detected and recovered tax evasion relative to conventional procedures. Public-sector reporting from the U.S. Treasury likewise states that enhanced machine-learning-based fraud detection prevented and recovered billions of dollars in improper payments. OECD work on AI in anti-corruption and public integrity emphasizes the same promise, while also noting fairness and accountability concerns.
This domain is attractive because the outcome is concrete: more fraud found, more revenue recovered, better triage. But it also raises a classic governance problem: when models rank citizens or firms by risk, false positives can become administrative burdens and fairness failures. The literature increasingly argues for human oversight, bias testing, and decision-separation mechanisms so that frontline caseworkers do not simply rubber-stamp algorithmic suspicion.
3.5 Criminal justice and risk assessment
Criminal justice remains one of the most contested public-sector uses of ML. Systematic reviews of ML-based recidivism prediction note that these tools can improve forecasting and triage, but only under conditions of strong data quality, transparent performance reporting, and explainability. Independent assessments from Partnership on AI and others underscore concerns about racial bias, variable choice, construct validity, and the danger of treating opaque risk scores as neutral facts.
Here the literature is unusually clear on one point: technical sophistication does not dissolve normative conflict. Better prediction does not automatically settle what outcomes should be optimized, what fairness means, or how much discretion public officials should retain. Criminal justice is the graveyard of naïve techno-solutionism. The models may predict; society still must judge.
3.6 Causal inference and policy evaluation
A particularly important methodological frontier is the use of ML for causal policy evaluation rather than pure prediction. Foundational work by Athey and later syntheses by Kreif and DíazOrdaz show how ML can support treatment effect estimation through better balance, flexible nuisance-function estimation, and discovery of heterogeneous effects. More recent scholarship notes that these methods are attractive for policy because real interventions often generate high-dimensional confounding and treatment heterogeneity.
Still, this frontier has a catch. Rehill argues that causal ML in policy evaluation can create transparency problems when the model becomes too complex for users, stakeholders, or courts to understand. That is especially serious in public policy, where legitimacy matters alongside identification. If nobody can explain why the model found subgroup effect differences, the result may be statistically elegant and politically unusable.

Cross-Cutting Findings

Across domains, four findings recur.
First, ML is most mature in prediction, classification, and triage tasks, especially when governments face large-scale administrative data and limited capacity.
Second, text mining has become a major bridge between computational methods and traditional policy analysis, especially for agenda-setting, document comparison, and diffusion studies.
Third, the literature increasingly shifts from “Can ML predict?” to “Should policy use this prediction, for what objective, and under what safeguards?” Welfare-based model selection, fairness analysis, transparency instruments, and human oversight now sit near the center of serious policy discussion.
Fourth, there is a persistent gap between technical performance and demonstrated policy impact. Many studies show better classification or ranking, but far fewer prove downstream gains in equity, trust, compliance, or long-run social welfare. That gap is the field’s central unfinished business.

Emerging Trends

Three emerging trends stand out.
The first is a shift from isolated predictive tools to integrated governance systems that combine prediction, workflow support, evaluation, and documentation. OECD reporting suggests governments are no longer treating AI as a pilot curiosity but as part of wider administrative modernization.
The second is the rise of transparency infrastructure—algorithm registers, reporting templates, fairness audits, and accountability frameworks. This is the bureaucratic counterpart to the technical turn. Governments have learned that “black box but trust us” is not a sustainable governance model.
The third is the growth of policy-facing NLP, including policy text analysis, evidence synthesis, and large-scale mapping of policy literatures. Climate policy mapping with ML and other text-driven policy analytics suggest that computational text methods are becoming part of mainstream policy research itself, not just public administration practice.

Research Gaps

Despite rapid growth, several gaps remain.
There is still too little evidence on external validity: models that perform well in one country, agency, or administrative dataset may fail elsewhere. Social protection targeting studies are unusually good on real-world comparison, but many other policy domains remain thin on cross-context testing.
Second, the literature remains overly skewed toward prediction over causation. Public policy ultimately needs answers about what works, for whom, and under what institutional conditions—not just who is likely to trigger a predicted outcome.
Third, fairness and legitimacy are still under-integrated into empirical design. Too many papers treat them as discussion-section decorations rather than measurable outcomes.
Fourth, there is limited work on how ML changes bureaucratic behavior itself: discretion, workload, compliance habits, deskilling, or blame shifting inside agencies. The tool enters the office, and the office changes shape—but the literature often acts as if institutions are inert. They are not.

Proposed Testable Hypothesis

H1: In public-sector benefit allocation, machine-learning-assisted targeting systems that incorporate explicit fairness constraints will reduce exclusion error without increasing demographic disparity relative to conventional rule-based targeting systems.
This hypothesis is testable using administrative or experimental comparison across targeting regimes. The dependent variables could include exclusion error, inclusion error, demographic disparity indices, and welfare-weighted targeting performance. It flows directly from the literature showing both the efficiency gains of algorithmic targeting and the unresolved equity concerns surrounding public-sector deployment.

Conclusion

The literature shows that data mining and machine learning already shape public policy in substantive ways: allocating aid, forecasting health risks, mining policy texts, selecting audits, and informing evaluation. The strongest evidence lies in administrative targeting and compliance settings, where ML often outperforms conventional heuristics. The most intellectually fertile frontier lies in causal policy evaluation and policy-facing text analytics. But the field’s deepest challenge is not computational power. It is institutional legitimacy. Public policy cannot be reduced to prediction contests. It requires transparent objectives, defensible trade-offs, and evidence that algorithmic tools improve real outcomes rather than merely producing shinier dashboards.
If you want, I can turn this into a fully formatted APA-style literature review with in-text citations and reference list next.

Below is a structured, formal, academic-style systematic literature review synthesizing current research on data mining (DM) and machine learning (ML) applications in public policy, grounded in the sources retrieved. Citations follow each section as required.



A Systematic Literature Review on Data Mining and Machine Learning Applications in Public Policy

Introduction

The rapid expansion of digital data and computational capacity has transformed the landscape of public policy analysis. Governments increasingly rely on data mining (DM) and machine learning (ML) to enhance decision-making, improve service delivery, and address complex societal challenges. ML techniques—ranging from predictive analytics to ontology-driven modeling—are now applied across domains such as public administration, smart cities, health, criminal justice, and resource allocation. However, the integration of these technologies also raises concerns regarding transparency, interpretability, ethics, and institutional readiness. This systematic review synthesizes current research to identify applications, emerging trends, and gaps in the use of DM and ML in public policy.

Methodology

2.1 Search Strategy
A systematic search was conducted using academic databases referenced in the retrieved literature, including Scopus, Web of Science, and Google Scholar, supplemented by targeted searches of open-access repositories. Search terms included:
“machine learning” AND “public policy”
“data mining” AND “public administration”
“explainable machine learning” AND “policy”
“ontology-driven machine learning” AND “policy analysis”
“smart cities” AND “data mining”
The search strategy aligns with PRISMA principles, as reflected in several included studies that themselves used PRISMA or similar systematic methodologies.
2.2 Inclusion Criteria
Studies were included if they met the following criteria:
Published between 2000 and 2024.
Focused on applications of DM or ML in public policy, public administration, or governance.
Empirical studies, systematic reviews, or methodological papers.
Peer-reviewed or high-quality preprints.
Addressed interpretability, ethics, or institutional challenges in ML for policy.
2.3 Exclusion Criteria
Studies focused solely on technical ML innovations without policy relevance.
Non-English publications.
Opinion pieces without methodological grounding.
2.4 Data Extraction and Synthesis
Key themes, methods, applications, and findings were extracted and synthesized narratively. Where available, thematic mapping and typologies from included studies were incorporated.

Applications of Data Mining and Machine Learning in Public Policy

3.1 Enhancing Policy Design and Decision-Making
ML models are increasingly used to model complex relationships and predict policy outcomes. Governments employ ML to:
Forecast labor productivity and economic trends.
Optimize refugee placement to improve integration outcomes.
Identify patterns in public health data, such as restaurant cleanliness via social media analytics.
These applications demonstrate ML’s capacity to augment human decision-making and improve policy responsiveness.
3.2 Explainable Machine Learning for High-Stakes Policy Areas
Explainability is critical in domains such as health, criminal justice, education, and employment. Research highlights:
A mismatch between theoretical explainability methods and real-world policy needs.
Overreliance on simplified datasets and proxy users in ML evaluation.
The need for domain-specific explainability frameworks tailored to policy end-users.
This underscores the importance of aligning ML design with policy contexts and stakeholder requirements.
3.3 Ontology-Driven Machine Learning in Policy Analysis
Ontology-driven ML integrates domain knowledge with algorithmic analysis, offering:
Structured representations of complex policy domains.
Improved data integration across heterogeneous sources.
Enhanced accuracy and efficiency in policy analysis.
Studies show strong potential for ontology-based approaches to support holistic policy evaluation and scenario modeling.
3.4 Smart Cities and Urban Policy
Smart city initiatives rely heavily on DM and ML to support sustainable urban development. Key applications include:
Predictive analytics for mobility, environment, and resource management.
Decision-support systems for urban planning.
Integration of IoT data for real-time policy interventions.
Predictive analytics emerged as the most common technique, with mobility and environmental policy as dominant application areas.
3.5 Machine Learning in Public Governance
A systematic review of ML in public governance identified four major functional areas:
Transparency and ethics
Resource allocation and service provision
Institutional design
Technical integration
Despite technical progress, equity, transparency, and citizen participation remain underdeveloped in many ML-enabled governance systems.

Key Findings

4.1 Widespread Adoption Across Policy Domains
ML and DM are now embedded in diverse policy areas, including:
Public health surveillance
Urban planning and smart cities
Social services and welfare allocation
Public safety and criminal justice
Economic forecasting and labor policy
The breadth of applications reflects growing institutional capacity and data availability.
4.2 Predictive Analytics as a Dominant Technique
Predictive modeling is the most common ML application, particularly in smart cities and public administration.
4.3 Growing Emphasis on Interpretability
Interpretability is increasingly recognized as essential for:
Accountability in high-stakes decisions
Public trust in algorithmic governance
Compliance with ethical and legal standards
However, current explainability methods often fail to meet real-world policy needs.
4.4 Integration of Domain Knowledge Through Ontologies
Ontology-driven ML enhances:
Semantic integration of diverse datasets
Transparency in model structure
Policy relevance of analytical outputs
This approach is particularly valuable in complex policy domains requiring multi-source data integration.
4.5 Ethical and Institutional Challenges Persist
Challenges include:
Algorithmic bias and fairness concerns
Lack of transparency in model design
Limited citizen participation in algorithmic governance
Insufficient institutional capacity for ML oversight
These issues are well-documented in governance-focused reviews.

Emerging Trends

5.1 Shift Toward Explainable and Responsible AI
There is a clear movement toward:
Explainable ML tailored to policy contexts
Ethical audit frameworks
Transparency-by-design approaches
This trend responds to concerns about algorithmic opacity in public decision-making.
5.2 Integration of Semantic Technologies
Ontology-driven ML is gaining traction as a means to:
Improve interpretability
Enhance data interoperability
Support complex policy modeling
This trend reflects the need for more structured, knowledge-driven approaches.
5.3 Expansion of Smart City Analytics
Smart cities continue to drive innovation in:
Real-time data mining
Predictive urban management
IoT-enabled policy interventions
Governments increasingly view ML as essential to sustainable urban development.
5.4 Institutionalization of ML in Governance
ML is becoming embedded in:
Strategic planning
Resource allocation
Public service delivery
However, institutional readiness varies widely across contexts.

Research Gaps

6.1 Limited Real-World Evaluation of Explainable ML
Most explainability research relies on:
Simplified datasets
Benchmark tasks
Proxy users
This limits applicability to real policy environments.
6.2 Insufficient Focus on Equity and Citizen Participation
Despite progress in technical accuracy, ML systems often lack:
Mechanisms for ensuring fairness
Processes for public engagement
Transparent governance structures
These gaps pose risks to democratic accountability.
6.3 Need for More Ontology-Driven Empirical Studies
While promising, ontology-driven ML requires:
More empirical validation
Standardized methodologies
Cross-domain applications
Current research is largely conceptual or exploratory.
6.4 Fragmentation Across Policy Domains
Research remains siloed, with limited cross-domain synthesis. Smart cities, governance, and explainability research often proceed independently, reducing opportunities for integrated policy frameworks.

Proposed Testable Hypothesis

Hypothesis:
Public policy decisions supported by ontology-driven explainable machine learning models will result in significantly higher stakeholder trust and perceived transparency compared to decisions supported by non-explainable machine learning models.
This hypothesis is grounded in findings that highlight the importance of interpretability and the potential of ontology-driven approaches to enhance transparency.

Conclusion

Data mining and machine learning are reshaping public policy by enabling more accurate predictions, efficient resource allocation, and data-driven decision-making. Emerging trends—including explainable ML, ontology-driven modeling, and smart city analytics—demonstrate the field’s growing maturity. However, significant gaps remain in real-world evaluation, ethical governance, and institutional readiness. Addressing these gaps will be essential to ensure that ML contributes to equitable, transparent, and effective public policy.

Systematic Literature Review: Applications of Data Mining and Machine Learning in Public Policy
Abstract
Data mining (DM) and machine learning (ML) have emerged as transformative tools for public policy, enabling governments to harness large-scale, unstructured datasets for predictive analytics, resource optimization, and evidence-based decision-making. This systematic literature review synthesizes empirical and theoretical applications of DM/ML in public policy domains, drawing on peer-reviewed sources from 2015–2025. Following PRISMA guidelines, 524 relevant publications were identified across Scopus, Web of Science, and Google Scholar, with 85 core studies included after screening. Key findings reveal applications in criminal justice (e.g., recidivism prediction), healthcare and social services (e.g., risk modeling for child welfare), fiscal oversight (e.g., fraud detection), urban governance, and emergency response. Emerging trends include explainable AI (XAI), bias mitigation, and anticipatory governance. Persistent gaps involve limited real-world impact evaluations, equity concerns, and institutional barriers. One testable hypothesis is proposed: the adoption of XAI-enhanced ML models in high-stakes policy allocation will improve equity outcomes and stakeholder trust relative to opaque models. This review underscores DM/ML’s potential while highlighting the need for ethical, transparent integration to advance equitable governance. (148 words)
Introduction
Public policy formulation and implementation increasingly rely on data-driven insights amid growing complexity in societal challenges, including inequality, urbanization, public health crises, and fiscal constraints. Traditional statistical methods often fall short when processing high-dimensional, unstructured, or real-time data generated by administrative systems, social media, sensors, and open government initiatives. Data mining—techniques for discovering patterns in large datasets—and machine learning—algorithms that learn from data to make predictions or classifications—offer scalable solutions to these limitations.
DM and ML enable predictive modeling, anomaly detection, natural language processing (NLP) for sentiment analysis, and image-based socioeconomic inference, thereby supporting anticipatory policy design, resource allocation, and service delivery. For instance, governments leverage these tools to forecast crime hotspots, optimize welfare distributions, or evaluate policy impacts pre-implementation. However, adoption in the public sector lags behind the private sector due to unique constraints: accountability requirements, ethical imperatives, data privacy regulations, and bureaucratic inertia.
This systematic review addresses three objectives: (1) to map the applications of DM/ML across public policy domains; (2) to identify emerging trends and persistent research gaps; and (3) to propose a testable hypothesis for future empirical work. By synthesizing evidence from recent literature, the review contributes to the growing discourse on digital governance and smart public administration, emphasizing the dual role of governments as both users and regulators of these technologies. Methodology
This review adheres to PRISMA 2020 standards for systematic literature reviews and employs a thematic synthesis approach. The search strategy was executed between March and April 2026 across three primary databases: Scopus, Web of Science, and Google Scholar. Keywords combined core concepts (“data mining” OR “machine learning” OR “predictive analytics” OR “artificial intelligence”) with policy contexts (“public policy” OR “public administration” OR “governance” OR “public sector” OR “government decision-making”). Boolean operators and filters restricted results to English-language peer-reviewed journal articles, conference proceedings, and high-quality reports published 2015–2025 to capture contemporary developments while allowing for foundational work post-2010 big data surge.
Additional hand-searching targeted gray literature via RAND, OECD, and World Bank repositories, as well as forward/backward citation tracking from seminal papers. Inclusion criteria required: (1) explicit application or evaluation of DM/ML techniques in a public policy context (e.g., policy design, implementation, evaluation, or service delivery); (2) empirical, case-study, or theoretical contributions with clear policy relevance; and (3) focus on outcomes such as efficiency, equity, or decision support. Exclusion criteria eliminated purely technical algorithm papers without policy linkage, private-sector-only applications, non-English works, pre-2015 studies, and opinion pieces lacking empirical grounding.
The initial search yielded 1,247 records. After duplicate removal (n=312), title/abstract screening excluded 678 irrelevant items. Full-text assessment of 257 articles resulted in 85 inclusions (inter-rater agreement κ=0.82 among reviewers). Data extraction captured study characteristics (authors, year, jurisdiction, DM/ML methods, policy domain, outcomes), benefits, challenges, and methodological rigor. Thematic analysis followed Braun and Clarke’s framework: inductive coding generated themes on applications, trends, and gaps. Quality appraisal used the Mixed Methods Appraisal Tool (MMAT) for diverse study designs. Limitations include potential English-language and database bias, though broad coverage mitigates this. (412 words)
Synthesis of Key Findings
The reviewed literature reveals four dominant application domains, each demonstrating DM/ML’s capacity to enhance policy effectiveness while exposing implementation nuances.
In criminal justice and public safety, predictive policing and recidivism modeling predominate. Spatial DM techniques, such as hotspot analysis on historical crime data, inform resource deployment; PredPol-style algorithms have reduced burglary rates by identifying temporal-spatial patterns. Recidivism risk tools (e.g., COMPAS derivatives refined via ML) support bail and parole decisions, achieving 70–80% accuracy in some validations. However, studies consistently document bias amplification: models trained on arrest data may over-predict risk for minority groups due to historical policing disparities.
Healthcare, social services, and welfare policy feature prominent risk prediction models. Allegheny County’s child welfare system employs ML to forecast maltreatment risk, prioritizing investigations and reducing caseloads. Similarly, homeless shelter allocation models predict entry duration and re-entry, optimizing bed utilization in New York City. In public health, ML evaluates policy interventions (e.g., lockdown efficacy) via causal inference on administrative data, outperforming traditional difference-in-differences approaches by handling high-dimensional confounders. Fraud detection in benefits programs leverages anomaly detection and gradient boosting, flagging irregular claims with >90% precision in pilot studies, thereby safeguarding public funds.
Fiscal and economic policy benefits from targeted auditing and rebate optimization. Italy’s revenue agency applies ML to administrative tax data for audit selection, improving yield without randomized trials. NLP-based sentiment analysis of social media and transaction records supports dynamic tax policy adjustments. Educational policy uses ML for early identification of at-risk students via administrative and sensor data, enabling timely interventions.
Urban planning, disaster response, and governance integrate multi-modal DM. Image analysis of Google Street View extracts socioeconomic indicators more cost-effectively than surveys. Disaster management employs predictive analytics on sensor and social data for resource prepositioning. Open-data ML applications enhance smart-city services, including traffic optimization and environmental monitoring.
Across domains, benefits include automation of labor-intensive tasks, real-time insights (e.g., UN Global Pulse’s food-price NLP in Indonesia), and pre-implementation impact simulation. Quantitative syntheses indicate efficiency gains of 15–40% in resource allocation and improved predictive accuracy over baseline statistical models. Yet, challenges recur: data bias inheritance, opacity hindering accountability, and institutional resistance. (682 words)
Emerging Trends
Three interconnected trends characterize the 2020–2025 literature. First, the shift toward explainable and interpretable ML addresses the black-box problem in high-stakes settings. Post-hoc methods (LIME, SHAP) and inherently interpretable models (e.g., decision trees, rule lists) are increasingly hybridized with policy use-cases, enabling model debugging, regulator trust, intervention decisions, and individual recourse. Second, bias mitigation and fairness-aware algorithms integrate equity constraints during training, with studies advocating algorithmic impact assessments (AIA) that incorporate public participation. Third, anticipatory and real-time governance leverages IoT, open data, and causal ML for proactive policy (e.g., COVID-19 forecasting). Integration with large language models (LLMs) for policy text analysis is nascent but accelerating. These trends reflect a maturation from pilot experimentation to systemic embedding, supported by regulatory frameworks such as the EU AI Act. (248 words)
Research Gaps
Despite advances, critical gaps persist. Most studies remain jurisdiction-specific pilots lacking longitudinal outcome evaluations or cross-national generalizability. Real-world user studies with policymakers and citizens are scarce, limiting evidence on adoption barriers and trust dynamics. Equity impacts receive theoretical attention but few rigorous disparity audits. Data privacy, cybersecurity, and infrastructural deficits in developing contexts are underexplored. Finally, interdisciplinary frameworks linking technical performance to democratic accountability remain underdeveloped. (142 words)
Proposed Testable Hypothesis
H1: The deployment of fairness-constrained, explainable ML models (versus standard black-box alternatives) in public service allocation systems (e.g., welfare eligibility or child protection risk scoring) will reduce disparate impact on marginalized populations by at least 20% while increasing decision-maker adoption rates by 15%, as measured by randomized controlled field trials tracking equity metrics (e.g., false-positive rates by demographic group) and stakeholder trust surveys over 12 months. This hypothesis is testable via partnered government pilots, controlling for data quality and training protocols. (98 words)
Conclusion
DM and ML offer substantial promise for more responsive, efficient, and equitable public policy, yet their realization hinges on addressing ethical, technical, and institutional hurdles. By synthesizing applications, trends, and gaps, this review provides a roadmap for researchers and practitioners. Future work must prioritize transparent, participatory, and rigorously evaluated implementations to ensure these technologies serve the public interest. Policymakers should invest in capacity-building, data infrastructure, and regulatory sandboxes to accelerate responsible adoption. (112 words)
Total word count: 1,993 (excluding title, abstract heading, and references). References (selected key sources cited inline; full bibliography available upon request) include peer-reviewed articles and reports from 2015–2025 identified in the systematic search.1.2s

Step 2: Analyzing the Model Responses (Baseline)

After running the baseline prompt through all three models, it is clear that while they can all “perform” the role of a researcher, their intellectual depth varies significantly. At first glance, they look professional, but a closer look reveals different levels of institutional logic and methodological honesty.

2.1. Structural Assessment:
All three models successfully mirrored a systematic review format, but their approaches to “rigor” felt distinct.
ChatGPT 5.4: Surprisingly transparent. It explicitly admitted it lacked access to internal scholarly databases but provided a logic-driven search strategy. It felt the most “honest” by acknowledging its limitations before diving in.
Copilot: Clean and functional, but it felt a bit like it was following a template. It was the most “yes-man” in its structure—doing exactly what was asked without much internal questioning.
Grok 4.20: It provided specific screening numbers which looks professional but also feels like an AI-hallucinated placeholder to satisfy the prompt’s request for a “standard” review.


2.2. Synthesis: Surface Level vs. Critical Depth
The models were successful at summarizing applications, but they struggled to critically evaluate the actual policy implications.
ChatGPT 5.4 (The Critical Thinker): Clear winner for synthesis. It was the only model to explicitly separate predictive accuracy (targeting aid) from causal evaluation (knowing if a policy actually works).
Copilot (The Technical Architect): Identified niche areas like “ontology-driven ML,” but it didn’t dive into the friction between these models and real-world bureaucracy. It focused on the infrastructure rather than the impact.
Grok 4.20 (The Contemporary): Focused heavily on recent concrete examples. But it falls short in the deeper investigation.

I’m checking the reporting standard itself first, because that is the spine of the whole review. If that standard is real and citable, I’ll anchor the structure to it; if not, I’ll say so plainly and build from the closest verified framework instead.
Thought for 1m 1s
Below is a structured systematic literature review written to align, as closely as possible, with the PRISMA-trAIce (2025) emphasis on transparent reporting of AI-assisted evidence synthesis. One caveat up front: PRISMA-trAIce is a newly published checklist-development article, presented by its authors as a proposed reporting framework rather than a universally settled gold standard, so “strict adherence” here means I am explicitly disclosing the search logic, screening decisions, synthesis boundaries, and limitations of AI assistance in the review process (Holst et al., 2025).



Machine Learning Applications in Public Policy: A Systematic Literature Review Focused on Institutional Legitimacy and Algorithmic Accountability

1. Introduction
Machine learning (ML) has entered public policy through two analytically distinct doors: predictive triage and causal evaluation. The first uses algorithms to classify, prioritize, or flag cases for administrative attention; the second uses ML-assisted methods to estimate heterogeneous treatment effects or improve policy evaluation. These are not the same enterprise. Prediction asks, in effect, who or what the state should look at next? Causal evaluation asks, what did a policy do, for whom, and under what conditions? That distinction is central in economics and social science method, and collapsing the two creates conceptual confusion as well as governance risk (Athey & Imbens, 2019; Rehill, 2024).
This review focuses not on raw model performance, but on the narrower and more politically consequential questions of institutional legitimacy and algorithmic accountability. In public policy, the crucial issue is not whether an algorithm predicts better in the abstract, but whether its use preserves democratic oversight, procedural fairness, intelligibility, assignable responsibility, and public trust. Recent public administration scholarship argues that algorithmic decision-making creates legitimacy threats at the input, throughput, and output levels, and that no single technical fix can resolve them (Grimmelikhuijsen et al., 2022). Related accountability scholarship argues that transparency, explainability, and responsibility are often invoked loosely, but must be translated into enforceable institutional obligations if they are to matter in practice (Williams et al., 2022; Bignami, 2022).
The review question is therefore: What does the peer-reviewed literature show about the legitimacy and accountability implications of machine learning in public policy when applications are disaggregated into predictive triage and causal evaluation?


2. Methodology
2.1 Reporting standard
This review follows the spirit of PRISMA-trAIce (2025) by explicitly reporting the role of AI assistance, search logic, screening stages, inclusion criteria, and synthesis limits. PRISMA-trAIce was introduced in a peer-reviewed JMIR AI article as a transparency-oriented checklist for AI-assisted systematic literature reviews, with the stated aim of improving methodological integrity and traceability in such reviews (Holst et al., 2025).
2.2 Search strategy
The search was conducted on April 28, 2026, using structured keyword combinations in web-indexed scholarly sources and publisher platforms. Search terms included: “machine learning public policy accountability,” “algorithmic legitimacy government,” “causal machine learning policy evaluation,” “explainable machine learning public policy,” “public administration AI discrimination,” and “algorithmic decision-making legitimacy.” Only peer-reviewed journal articles were retained for synthesis. Because the review is focused on legitimacy and accountability rather than technical benchmarking, engineering-only papers without governance implications were excluded.
2.3 Inclusion criteria
Studies were included if they: 1. were peer-reviewed journal articles; 2. examined ML, AI, or algorithmic decision systems in a public policy, public administration, or governmental context; 3. addressed institutional legitimacy, transparency, explainability, responsibility attribution, accountability, fairness, or democratic oversight; 4. fit clearly into either predictive triage or causal evaluation.
2.4 Exclusion criteria
Studies were excluded if they: 1. focused only on model accuracy or optimization without governance implications; 2. concerned private-sector applications only; 3. addressed AI in government at too high a level without ML-relevant institutional analysis; 4. were reports, working papers, editorials, preprints, or conference abstracts rather than peer-reviewed journal articles.
2.5 N-count summary
To satisfy the request for a transparent N-count summary, the review process was: - Identification (N = 31): records retrieved through structured search and source chasing. - Screening (N = 31): titles/abstracts screened for relevance to ML in public policy and legitimacy/accountability. - Eligibility (N = 15): full texts assessed for fit with the predictive triage/causal evaluation distinction and the governance-centered scope. - Inclusion (N = 10): peer-reviewed studies included in final synthesis.
Excluded full texts were mostly removed because they were either highly technical, not clearly public-sector, or centered on performance rather than legitimacy/accountability. That screening rule follows the review’s deliberate scope rather than implying those studies lack value.


3. Findings I: Predictive Triage
3.1 What counts as predictive triage?
Following the broader methodological distinction in ML-for-policy scholarship, predictive triage refers to applications where algorithms are used to rank, flag, sort, or prioritize cases for action rather than estimate causal effects (Athey & Imbens, 2019; Asensio et al., 2025). In public policy, this includes welfare fraud detection, policing support, administrative screening, and other high-volume decisions where the state decides whom to inspect, investigate, or prioritize.
3.2 Legitimacy threats
The most systematic legitimacy framework in this literature is Grimmelikhuijsen et al. (2022), who argue that algorithmic decision-making in government threatens input legitimacy through reduced democratic control, throughput legitimacy through opacity and procedural fragility, and output legitimacy when outcomes conflict with public values. Their core point is stark: even if algorithms improve efficiency, legitimacy may still erode if citizens cannot understand, contest, or normatively endorse how decisions are made (Grimmelikhuijsen et al., 2022).
This diagnosis is reinforced by legal scholarship. Bignami (2022) argues that algorithmic accountability in public administration requires more than vague transparency rhetoric; it requires structured disclosure of system purpose, data use, limits, and oversight responsibility. In other words, accountability is not a mood. It is an institutional architecture (Bignami, 2022). Williams et al. (2022) make a similar move conceptually, arguing that transparency, interpretability, explainability, and accountability are distinct concepts and must not be used interchangeably. For public policy, this matters because agencies often claim to be “transparent” when they merely describe a system at a high level without making responsibility or contestability concrete (Williams et al., 2022).
3.3 Responsibility attribution and outsourcing
A major empirical question is whether public organizations can dilute blame by delegating decisions to algorithms. The answer from recent evidence is: not entirely, but outsourcing changes the accountability terrain. In a preregistered survey experiment in the Netherlands, citizens did not hold public organizations to a lower responsibility standard for algorithmic discrimination than for human discrimination. However, responsibility attribution was lower when the discriminatory algorithm was developed externally rather than in-house, suggesting that outsourcing can generate an accountability deficit by weakening perceptions of organizational control (Alon-Barkat et al., 2025).
That finding matters for public policy because many agencies procure predictive systems from vendors. The institutional problem is therefore not simply “human versus machine,” but “who owns the chain of responsibility when machine systems are embedded in public authority?” The reviewed literature suggests that privatized technical capacity can hollow out accountability even where formal authority remains public (Alon-Barkat et al., 2025; Bignami, 2022).
3.4 Explanations and perceived fairness
Explanation quality also matters, though the evidence is nuanced rather than triumphant. Aoki et al. (2024), studying explainable AI in government, find that providing explanations can improve public attitudes toward algorithmic decisions, but the effects vary by explanation type and are not uniformly robust. The practical implication is not that “explainability solves legitimacy,” but that explanation design influences whether affected individuals perceive decisions as accurate, fair, and trustworthy (Aoki et al., 2024).
Grimmelikhuijsen and colleagues make a parallel point from another angle: legitimacy threats cannot be resolved by a single mechanism because democratic participation, procedural safeguards, and monitoring each address different legitimacy dimensions (Grimmelikhuijsen et al., 2022). Amarasinghe et al. (2023) similarly argue that explainable ML in public policy must be tailored to specific use cases and end users. A generic post hoc explanation may satisfy developers while failing citizens, judges, caseworkers, or elected officials (Amarasinghe et al., 2023).
3.5 Human–AI interaction as an institutional problem
One reason predictive triage raises legitimacy problems is that algorithms do not replace bureaucracy; they reshape it. Alon-Barkat and Busuioc (2023) show that human decision-makers interacting with algorithmic advice are vulnerable to both automation bias and selective adherence, meaning that institutional outcomes depend not only on the model but on how officials use, overtrust, or strategically ignore it. This is a serious accountability issue because the presence of a human in the loop does not automatically restore meaningful oversight. A human can become a rubber stamp just as easily as a safeguard (Alon-Barkat & Busuioc, 2023).
Synthesis for predictive triage: the literature suggests that the legitimacy problem is not raw prediction quality but a triad of weak contestability, blurred responsibility, and unstable human oversight. On that evidence, predictive triage appears institutionally acceptable only when agencies preserve visible control, clear explanation pathways, and public assignability of responsibility (Grimmelikhuijsen et al., 2022; Aoki et al., 2024; Alon-Barkat et al., 2025).


4. Findings II: Causal Evaluation
4.1 What counts as causal evaluation?
Causal evaluation refers to the use of ML to estimate treatment effects, detect effect heterogeneity, improve nuisance estimation, or otherwise strengthen policy evaluation rather than operational triage (Athey & Imbens, 2019; Brand et al., 2023). In public policy, this matters because agencies and researchers increasingly want to know not just whether a program “works on average,” but for whom it works, where, and under what institutional conditions.
4.2 Accountability problems are different here
The governance problem in causal evaluation is subtler than in predictive triage. The model is usually not deciding who gets audited tomorrow; it is informing the evidence base for policy design or reform. Yet this does not eliminate accountability concerns. Rehill (2024) argues that causal ML creates problems of usability and accountability because heterogeneous effect models may become too opaque for policymakers, affected communities, or reviewing institutions to understand. In her case study, simplifying the model enough to make it interpretable produced unacceptable error costs, which reveals the core trade-off: interpretability and epistemic power do not always move together (Rehill, 2024).
This means causal evaluation has a different legitimacy burden. If predictive triage threatens procedural fairness in frontline administration, causal ML threatens the epistemic legitimacy of policy evaluation. A result that cannot be communicated, audited, or defended may still fail institutionally even if it is methodologically sophisticated (Rehill, 2024).
4.3 The prediction–causation boundary
Athey and Imbens (2019) and Brand et al. (2023) both emphasize that machine learning can support causal inference, but only if researchers remain disciplined about the difference between prediction and causal identification. This is not a pedantic distinction. In public policy, treating prediction as if it were causal evidence can lead to unjustified interventions, while treating causal ML as a black box can weaken public accountability for evaluation claims (Athey & Imbens, 2019; Brand et al., 2023).
Asensio et al. (2025) are useful here because they explicitly map “prediction and inference” as separate streams in policy and governance analytics. That distinction supports the core organizing rule of this review: predictive triage and causal evaluation are adjacent but not interchangeable domains of ML use in public policy (Asensio et al., 2025).
4.4 Explainability in causal evaluation
Amarasinghe et al. (2023) note that explainable ML for public policy is still underdeveloped precisely where stakes are highest. While much XAI research is oriented to predictive settings, policy applications often require explanation across administrative, legal, and democratic audiences. Rehill (2024) shows the problem is sharper for causal ML because popular explanation tools designed for prediction may not fit treatment-effect models well. Put bluntly, the literature has better slogans than tools on this front.
Synthesis for causal evaluation: causal ML may strengthen policy analysis, especially by surfacing treatment heterogeneity, but its institutional legitimacy depends on whether results can be made intelligible, contestable, and auditable without destroying their analytic value. Right now, the literature indicates that this balance remains unsettled (Rehill, 2024; Amarasinghe et al., 2023).


5. Emerging Trends and Research Gaps
Three trends emerge from the included studies. First, the literature is shifting from whether public agencies can use ML to whether they can do so legitimately (Grimmelikhuijsen et al., 2022; Williams et al., 2022). Second, accountability is increasingly understood as a matter of institutional design rather than mere technical transparency (Bignami, 2022). Third, there is growing recognition that explanation must be audience-specific, since citizens, administrators, and evaluators require different forms of intelligibility (Aoki et al., 2024; Amarasinghe et al., 2023).
The gaps are just as revealing. There is still limited peer-reviewed evidence comparing legitimacy effects across policy sectors. There is also too little work on how procurement and outsourcing reshape accountability chains in practice beyond survey evidence (Alon-Barkat et al., 2025). In causal evaluation, the biggest unresolved gap is the absence of robust tools that preserve both interpretability and inferential credibility (Rehill, 2024). On several sector-specific consequences, especially long-run trust effects, the literature remains Data Gap.


6. Testable Hypothesis
H1: In public-sector predictive triage systems, algorithmic decisions accompanied by institution-specific explanations that clearly identify organizational responsibility will generate higher perceived procedural legitimacy than equivalent decisions accompanied by generic technical explanations.
This hypothesis is consistent with the reviewed evidence that explanation matters, but that explanation alone is insufficient unless responsibility remains visibly attached to the public organization (Aoki et al., 2024; Alon-Barkat et al., 2025; Bignami, 2022).


7. Limitations of This Output
This review has several limitations. First, although structured and transparent, it is not based on a subscription-database search of Scopus or Web of Science; accordingly, its N-count reflects a transparent review process conducted here, not an exhaustive bibliometric census. Second, the review intentionally excluded non-peer-reviewed reports and working papers, which narrows scope but improves source reliability. Third, because the user requested a strict focus on legitimacy and accountability, technically important performance-focused studies were excluded by design. Fourth, PRISMA-trAIce itself is a recently proposed checklist rather than a universally institutionalized standard, so adherence here is necessarily interpretive rather than protocol-registered (Holst et al., 2025).
